1: Jehoahaz son of Jehu begins to reign over Israel in Samaria in the twenty-third year of Joash of Judah. The synchronism ties the two kingdoms together chronologically through the Jehu dynasty and the Joash interregnum.
2: wayyēleḵ bəḥaṭṭôʾṯ Yārābəʿām — 'he walked in the sins of Jeroboam.' The fixed formulaic indictment of every northern king since 1 Kings 12:30; its repetition signals that Jeroboam's golden calves function as a permanent structural sin of the northern kingdom, not merely individual apostasies.
3: YHWH's anger burns against Israel and he gives them into the hand of Hazael king of Aram and Ben-hadad son of Hazael — the two successive rulers of the Aramean empire who dominated Israel through most of Jehu's dynasty. The punishment is YHWH's direct action through foreign military pressure.
4: wayyəḥal Yəhôʾāḥāz ʾeṯ-pənê YHWH — 'Jehoahaz implored/sought the face of YHWH.' ḥālāh pānîm (to make the face of YHWH) is an idiom for earnest petition; ḥālāh literally means to be weak or sick, idiomatically to approach in humility. YHWH listened because he saw the oppression of Israel by the king of Aram — divine mercy operates on compassion for human suffering, not exclusively on merit.
5: wayyitten YHWH lə yiśrāʾēl môšîaʿ — 'YHWH gave Israel a deliverer/savior.' môšîaʿ is the participle of yāšaʿ, the same root as Yeshua; the historical deliverer here is likely an Assyrian king (Adad-nirari III pressed Aram) rather than an Israelite figure — YHWH's salvation instruments can be pagan powers. Israel dwelt in its tents as before, i.e., returned to normality; yet the sin of Jeroboam continued (v6).
6: Despite YHWH's deliverance, Israel did not depart from the house of Jeroboam. The ʾăšērāh (Asherah pole) still stood in Samaria — a visible symbol of syncretism at the capital. Deliverance from oppression did not produce repentance; the structural cult persisted.
7: Jehoahaz's remaining military force is reduced to fifty horsemen, ten chariots, ten thousand foot soldiers — kî ʾibbaḏām melek ʾărām wayyəśimēm kəʿāpār lāḏuš, 'because the king of Aram destroyed them and made them like dust for threshing.' The image of threshed dust conveys near-total pulverization; Israel's military capacity is barely functional.
8: The rest of Jehoahaz's acts — 'his might' (gəḇûrāṯô) and all he did — are in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel. The standard archival reference; the irony is that the chapter documenting his military humiliation must appeal to a record that uses the term 'might.'
9: Jehoahaz dies and is buried in Samaria; his son Joash (Jehoash) reigns in his place. The brief dynastic succession notice moves quickly to the more theologically significant reign of Jehoash, who will have the encounter with the dying Elisha.
10: Jehoash son of Jehoahaz begins to reign over Israel in Samaria in the thirty-seventh year of Joash of Judah, reigning sixteen years. The synchronism with Joash of Judah (a different Joash) produces a confusing name-overlap that the narrative handles by keeping strict synchronistic tracking.
11: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bəʿênê YHWH lōʾ-sār mikkol-ḥaṭṭôʾṯ Yārābəʿām — the standard negative formula; Jehoash of Israel follows Jeroboam's sins despite being the king who will be instructed by the dying Elisha and who will achieve military victories against Aram.
12: The rest of Jehoash's acts include his 'might' and his war with Amaziah of Judah — a proleptic reference to the conflict described in 2 Kings 14. The archival notice also appears in full in 2 Kings 14:15, suggesting the compiler has integrated the Elisha deathbed episode between the two archival references to Jehoash.
13: Jehoash dies and is buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; Jeroboam II sits on his throne. The dynasty continues, but the text is about to look back at the extraordinary deathbed scene with Elisha before moving to Jeroboam II's long, prosperous reign.
14: Elisha falls ill with the illness from which he will die — in contrast to Elijah's translation without death (2 Kings 2:11), Elisha dies of sickness. Joash king of Israel comes down to him and weeps over his face, crying ʾāḇî ʾāḇî rekeḇ yiśrāʾēl ûpārāšāyw — 'my father, my father, the chariots of Israel and its horsemen.' This is verbatim Elisha's cry for Elijah in 2 Kings 2:12; Joash uses the same words that Elisha used, applying the title of prophetic intercessor now to Elisha himself. The prophet was Israel's real military defense — more than chariots.
15: Elisha instructs Joash to take a bow and arrows; the king takes them. The instruction moves from mourning to symbolic action — the deathbed becomes a prophetic stage.
16: Elisha instructs Joash to put his hand on the bow; Elisha then lays his own hands on the king's hands — the prophet's hands covering the king's hands on the bow is a transfer of prophetic authority to royal military action, a laying-on-of-hands impartation for the coming campaign.
17: ḥēṣ-tešûʿāh laYHWH wəḥēṣ-tešûʿāh bə ʾărām — 'arrow of YHWH's salvation (tešûʿāh) and arrow of salvation against Aram.' tešûʿāh derives from yāšaʿ, the same root as Yeshua/Joshua — salvation-vocabulary infuses the military prophecy; Elisha declares total victory: Joash will strike Aram at Aphek until he has finished them.
18: Elisha tells Joash to strike the arrows on the ground; the king strikes three times and stops. Elisha is angry: ləhakkôṯ ḥāmêš ʾô-šēš pəʿāmîm — 'you should have struck five or six times, then you would have struck Aram until you finished it; now you will strike Aram only three times.' The king's half-committed response limits the scope of YHWH's victory; incomplete faith produces incomplete deliverance.
19: The three limited strikes translate into three limited military victories over Aram — the prophet's word binds the event; the king's action in the prophetic drama predetermined the outcome. The lesson is not that YHWH's power is limited but that the human receptacle limits what YHWH will pour into it.
20: Elisha dies and is buried. The notice is stark: wayyāmāṯ ʾĕlîšāʿ wayyiqbəruhû — 'and Elisha died and they buried him.' The contrast with Elijah is total: no chariot, no fire, no translation — just death and burial. Yet the death leads immediately to the postmortem miracle of v21.
21: wayyəhî hêm qōḇərîm ʾîš wəhinnēh rāʾû ʾeṯ-haggəḏûḏ wayyašlîḵû ʾeṯ-hāʾîš bəqeḇer ʾĕlîšāʿ wayyēleḵ wayyiggaʿ hāʾîš bəʿaṣmôṯ ʾĕlîšāʿ wayyəḥî wayyāqom ʿal-ragəlāyw — the syntax tumbles with the urgency of the event: they were burying a man, saw raiders, threw the body into Elisha's tomb; the man touched Elisha's bones, wayyəḥî wayyāqom — 'he lived and he stood up on his feet.' The two verbs ḥāyāh (live) and qûm (rise) are resurrection vocabulary; the dead bones of YHWH's prophet still carry his prophetic charge. This is the last Elisha story in Kings.
22: Hazael king of Aram oppressed Israel all the days of Jehoahaz — a retrospective summary that frames the entire Jehoahaz-to-Jehoash period under Aramean military pressure. The oppression is the context against which YHWH's compassion and the Elisha prophecies operate.
23: wayyəḥunnêm wayyeraḥămêm wayyipen ʾălêhem lǝmaʿan bərîṯô ʾeṯ-ʾaḇrāhām yiṣḥāq wəyaʿăqōḇ — 'YHWH was gracious to them, had compassion on them, and turned to them for the sake of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.' Three divine verbs: ḥānan (grace), riḥam (compassion/mercy), pānāh (turn/face). YHWH's preservation of Israel is explicitly covenantal — not because Israel deserves it but because YHWH bound himself to Abraham; the Abrahamic covenant underwrites discipline-era Israel's survival.
24: Hazael king of Aram dies; Ben-hadad his son reigns in his place. The death of Israel's greatest oppressor opens the possibility for the three Elisha-prophesied victories Jehoash will now achieve against the less powerful Ben-hadad III.
25: Jehoash defeats Ben-hadad three times — exactly the three times prophesied by Elisha's arrow-striking ritual (v18-19). wayyāšeḇ ʾeṯ-ʿārê yiśrāʾēl — 'he restored the cities of Israel,' specifically the cities that Hazael had taken from Jehoahaz. Prophetic word becomes historical event precisely as the prophetic act signified; the limitation was the king's three strikes, not YHWH's capacity.
